core_behavior.note: All three models describe secure payment processing with confidentiality and integrity, but none flags that 'securely' is undefined or that direct card handling vs third-party processor is an unresolved architectural assumption.

inputs.note: Model B provides exhaustive typed field definitions with constraints, Model A lists high-level input categories, and Model C provides a moderate-detail list, reflecting different design choices not constrained by the requirement.

outputs.note: Model B has an empty outputs array while Models A and C both specify transaction confirmations, IDs, receipts, and error messages.

assumptions.note: Model B has an empty assumptions array while Models A and C both specify third-party gateway usage and PCI-DSS compliance; the critical architectural assumption of direct card handling vs third-party processor is present in A and C but absent in B.

failure_modes.note: Model B has an empty failure_modes array while Models A and C specify retry logic, error messaging, and logging behaviors.

edge_cases.note: Model B has an empty edge_cases array while Models A and C enumerate cases such as duplicate transactions, invalid instruments, and insufficient funds.

security_constraints.note: All three models populate security constraints but with different scopes: Model C adds input sanitization against SQL injection/XSS and rate limiting, Model A adds access controls and audits, and Model B focuses narrowly on tokenization and TLS; none explicitly flags that 'securely' is undefined.

performance_constraints.note: Model B has an empty performance_constraints array while Model A specifies under 5 seconds and Model C specifies under 2 seconds with additional throughput and availability metrics.

summary: OMI was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 4 of the 8 fields, driven primarily by Model B's empty arrays for outputs, assumptions, failure_modes, and edge_cases.
